text: The surgeon performs an endoscopy to visually confirm the location of the tumor within the stomach.
The radially dilating Versastep devices are seen entering the stomach under endoscopic visualization, with the intragastric ports clearly visible.
Robotic ports are placed over the Versastep ports on the stomach wall, utilizing the intragastric ports to enhance traction during the procedure.
The surgeon insufflates the stomach to fifteen millimeters of mercury, visibly expanding the organ under endoscopic visualization.

The surgeon uses fenestrated Maryland forceps and Hot Shears to perform a complete submucosal resection of the gastroesophageal junction mass, carefully dissecting the tissue under endoscopic visualization.
The surgeon initiates dissection in the submucosal plane, focusing on the area surrounding the GE junction mass.
The endoscopic view reveals a sizable lobulated mass at the gastroesophageal junction, visible within the insufflated stomach.
The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the tumor at the GE junction, taking care to avoid disrupting the mass.
Cigarette gauze is used for retraction and blotting during the surgical procedure, aiding in maintaining a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses hot shears to continue the dissection and control hemostasis, ensuring precise removal of tissue while maintaining a clear surgical field.
The robotic arms maneuver with precision to maintain the dissection just above the gastric muscles, demonstrating their agility in navigating the surgical field.
The surgeon uses bipolar or fenestrated Marylins to cauterize areas of bleeding encountered during the procedure, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a five-millimeter endocatch bag, inserted through the robotic ports, to remove the specimen after the mass has been excised from the submucosa.
The surgeon utilizes an endoscope to assist in the procedure, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The endoscope is introduced with a snare, which securely grasps the end of the five-millimeter endocatch bag to facilitate specimen removal.
The endoscope is used to remove the specimen bag transorally, completing the extraction process.
The surgeon closes the mucosal defect in two layers using a 3-0 Maxon V-lock suture, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon ties the two ends of the V-lock suture together to secure the closure of the mucosal defect.
The surgeon inserts the endoscope to visually inspect the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring there is no narrowing or obstruction.
Under direct endoscopic visualization, the robotic arms are carefully withdrawn from the intragastric ports.
The surgeon desufflates the stomach, reducing its volume, and then insufflates the abdomen, increasing the intra-abdominal space.
The surgeon closes the intragastric ports using interrupted sutures with 3-0 silk, securing the closure of the ports.
The surgeon performs a leak test by insufflating air endoscopically through the stomach, observing for any signs of air escape or leakage.
